speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about two people who had escaped--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two people escaped from Auschwitz, two men who dug their way out under the wire-- under the electric fence. And I don't-- I knew one of them. I knew his name. I no longer remember his name. They also took with them maps of the compound, of Auschwitz, of the railroad tracks. And they were going to make the case if they get out. And as punishment for their attempt-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] I keep on talking-- as punishment for their attempt, we were standing appell all that day without food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they didn't-- they weren't successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were looking for them. They noticed that two people had broken out, and they started counting us. And they established who it was. And everywhere else everybody was standing in formation, and they kept on counting us. And, by the way, the people that did the counting and that were in charge of-- of-- of this, some of them were very primitive. They didn't know how to count. There was a-- a Romanian volks soldier, volks, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were prisoners as well. Were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were-- very many of them volunteered to work in the concentration camps. They were no friends of ours. And he kept on counting till 10, and then he started again. And those were our overseers, where we had highly educated people, sophisticated people standing, in their garb there, being counted. When these two people broke out, of course, we were rooting for them. And we-- we knew that if the Allies would respond to our request to-- to bomb the railroad tracks, they would probably kill us. But we knew that. That didn't really enter the picture. The-- the idea was to stop the slaughter, to slow it down at least. And save as many people as we can as they were coming in. And they had maps, and they risked their lives. They made it to freedom. The partisans picked them up, and we know from after the war that these maps were delivered and never acted upon. Nothing happened. The railroad tracks were never bombed. But we were on our way out of Auschwitz, and after some hours, maybe it was a day, somebody crawled up to look out that little window and said, we must be in Germany. It-- the signs say it's Germany. And they lifted me up on their shoulders, and I looked out. Yes it is German. I was totally bilingual. I understood what it was saying. And I was totally overwhelmed. Suddenly you see green lawns and flowers, and it must have been Sunday, because people were going to church, obviously in their finery. And flowers growing, and-- and-- and life going on while we were buried alive there. It-- it just brought it back to me. You see, and-- while you are in-- in-- in such a situation, not that anyone can imagine what it's like, you make nice little compartments out of the day in order to survive. You say this hour, that hour, we eat now, and it's going to be fine. And you say just if I get through this long walk in my shoes, the-- the-- the wooden shoes, I'll be fine. So you don't look at a day. You look at an hour. You look at a small package of time. And this really shocked us. We took turns looking out that window and seeing normal life, manicured lawns, people sitting at a picnic table, or whatever it was there. And-- and this didn't seem right.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was very, very disturbing. How can you rejoin such a world after what you've seen? And-- and you feel this-- this obligation. There were about 600 of us women who left Auschwitz together.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were liberated, and later on, we were down to about 150 from our original group. But we arrived in Hamburg. That was a harbor, Hamburg harbor. And after that, we were-- they changed our-- our location every few months, because that is their system. You don't become familiarized with anyone or any place. You just keep on moving around. We arrived there, and the-- the bombardments were going on every day and every night. At night, it was the British. And at the daytime it was the Americans. And --

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at this point? It was a camp, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was an-- a-- some kind of a-- of a-- the-- the underground of a railroad station, or a-- some kind of a, I don't know, a storage place where they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a warehouse type--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what it was. It was just a huge place, which also-- we had-- and connected to it were shelters, bomb shelters, to which they forced us to go. I mean, we had so little sleep because they counted us from morning to night. And the-- this location we arrived at, and there were tiered bunk beds, you know. Three on top of each other. And we started doing cleanup work after the bombardments. When the bombardments stopped, woke us up at 3:00 o'clock in the morning to-- to go to work at 9:00, and counted us. And we went by-- by boat. There was some kind of a ferry to the other side, and we cleaned up, cleared the roads from the bombardment. Sometimes the bricks were boiling hot. And we made a chain, and we threw it--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was all a group of women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Women and girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: We never saw our men again. And we-- we did this work, and weary. We got some food. It was not very much. It was less than before. As things got worse for the Germans and the Allies were advancing, it became worse. And the bombings went on all the time, and we had to clear these things away. And going up on the truck to go back-- that's on the ferry. That's to the-- the-- they-- they drove us to the ferry. On the way back again after a day's work. And my mother sometimes had trouble getting up on the truck, and we went behind her and helped her. She was going through her change of life, and she had this mission to get us through that. She felt somehow that she was our backbone. And she was. She really was. She had such standards, and such dignity, and such wisdom. It was be too much to enumerate that. I-- we-- when we changed over to the next location, I remember, we-- it was a new-- a new camp. We had to fill our sacks to-- that we slept on, and it had been a very tiring day. We had walked for hours. And there was one faucet with a trickle of water. And we lined up to drink and to wash our faces and hands. And other people lined up in the meantime for their food. They were giving out food. And when we got our turn, this-- they had run out of soup. The soup was usually a watery concoction with some turnip floating around. And it was no-- no chance of eating anything non kosher. It was water and a potato. Well, they had run out of the soup, and all we got was a boiled potato in the skin. And people wolfed it down and ate it. And she said, children, let's sit down and eat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Takes the same time. And there were some benches there on the side. So we sat down, and she said, you know what? We're not going to let the Germans win. We're going to peel the potato.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're not going to be less hungry if you eat the peel, and you see how thin that peel is. They sometimes get at the difference between human and animal, is-- is that thickness. And let's show them. We'll peel the potato. It lasts us longer that way too. And we thought, oh, it was all in good fun and looked at her and felt we have to go along with this. So we peeled our potato. Somebody ate the peel immediately. It didn't really make a difference, but it is-- it is-- it's a tremendous thing to remember. And this is the kind of strings that-- and-- and-- and standards that she kept on reminding us of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't eat the peels that day. And we were changed to various places, and as time went on and things got worse, the reports came through occasionally. In one of the camps that we went to, my sister was tall for her age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they picked two or three tall girls to go to the next camp, which was-- there were some prisoners of war there. And they were supposed to bring some stones on a wheelbarrow. And so, that's why there had to be three of them to help to-- to make a sidewalk where we were working, where we were staying. And she came-- they came back, those three, and they said-- my sister said, on the bottom of the stones is a package for you. And that was, again, my contacts that knew we--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were able to find--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: --they didn't. It was mis-delivered to these people who-- who were wondering whether it was one of us, because, first of all, it was a girl's name. Secondly, it wasn't-- they were French there, and so in came this packaging, and there was flour in it. It was all that was left. Obviously, there had been a lot more in it. And there was a letter. There was a letter that they-- that they got indirect knowledge of us and that we're together, and that we should keep our-- our spirits up. And the war is going to be over soon and all that. And my mother took that flour and mixed it with water and made [NON-ENGLISH] out of it. Miraculous, and the whole room where we were staying together partook of that. It was like a party. It wasn't filling or anything, but everyone that was a-- from outside. And there was another experience we had there. We had become-- we knew each other very well by that time. We were only about 20 people in that one room, all women that had started out together, many of them from Theresienstadt. And we-- we knew each other well. We helped each other. We warned each other. It was-- it had crystallized into a good group. And somehow, one or two people from outside, they had no room in the other room, so they brought them to us. And there was a problem. Bread was disappearing. And the rations, things were missing. And I saw justice administered. I mean, I was-- I was on the top tier, because I was young and I was able to climb up. And they were going to find out who that is. And they told me to keep my mouth shut, that they were going to take care of it. And as everyone had settled down for the night, they switched on the light and said, everyone stand next to their bunk. And two of the women examined. Anything you owned was on your bed, or under it. And they found this extra bread in this one woman's, and they said we weren't-- we're going to punish you. They threw her out. They said, get out, find your way somewhere else, and don't ever come near us again. And that was very interesting. But you see, bread wasn't just a piece of bread. It was the difference between life and death. This-- this-- we were-- our health and our being was so finely balanced that this one bread ration may have you know, pushed somebody over the top. And it could not be permitted. And there were always some rotten apples in every group. Springing back to Auschwitz, when I was thinking of bread, my father didn't eat bread in Auschwitz on Pesach. He had not [NON-ENGLISH] at that point anymore. He had no [NON-ENGLISH] He knew a lot by heart. And he said, I forbid you to do the same thing. He told us what he was going to do. You had to find a gentile. You couldn't do it to a Jew, give bread to a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, he forbid you not to eat the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to eat the bread. You must eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you must eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And must nourish yourself, and you must remember at the same time why you're doing it. And he didn't eat bread. He changed his bread ration for all of Pesach for six little potatoes, tiny ones. That's what he ate.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's for a-- for a whole week.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's for a whole week, he did not. He didn't give in, he-- for his own standards. He didn't want us to do it. Definitely not. Where were we? We were in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- that's finished, and now you're back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the-- when the woman you were telling about, the woman--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who was thrown out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found that very interesting, that-- that they had strength and the, what should I say, the determination not to get contaminated by that one person who was doing that. And then started the way-- things were really going bad in the war, very badly. We started-- They told us that they were going to move us inland. And that we have to leave there. And on the way-- on the way out of there, never had-- I hadn't, all the years, some French prisoner of war called out-- I was walking near the fence-- and threw me something. It was a piece of good soap. I really appreciated that. Everybody had a try at it. There were other-- there was another time where we were in one of these German camps where it was-- we-- the water-- the water didn't work. The water was cut off, and we couldn't wash. And my mother was very particular about hygiene, and she encouraged us not to let go on that, because if you give in, you are really lost. So there was no water. And she said, you know, there's fresh snow outside. Take a pail, there's a pail there, and collect that fresh snow and take it in to where the showers are. And it was cold. And we did it. We washed with snow, and it was very exhilarating, it-- because-- and-- and you were not exposed in any way because there was a cloud around you from your body heat. And we washed systematically, and-- and it was snow. I mean you keep on going back to different things. Its-- it doesn't make sense, but we were partially walking, and partially traveling on trucks, and partially by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the-- the names of the different-- any of the places? Because you say you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --went from place to place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you mention a few of the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'll write it down. I wrote it down because I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last thing you mentioned was when you came to Hamburg and-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were-- and there were all kind of factories there. We arrived in Hamburg, and where we stayed was called am Dessauer Ufer. Ufer is a shore. And there was a-- a factory called Wilhelmsburg [NON-ENGLISH]. The reason I have all these names is because I tried to claim restitution, which I'm not getting. I-- they gave me a lump sum after the war where they figured out from the day I started wearing the star to the time of liberation, I had been deprived of my freedom and my education for 42 months and 26 days. They paid me for the 42 months, about roughly $30 a month. And the 26 days they didn't pay me for because it wasn't a complete month. It was about $2,500 in our money, and we didn't really want the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave half of it to our synagogue, of which we are founders, and commemorated my parents' names there. And the other bit that was left after that I blew on a used car. And I really enjoyed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, there was Wilhelmsburg [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a factory that-- that employed slave labor, women. And there was the [NON-ENGLISH] Deutsche et Ol Werke. Et Ol is oil. They had slave labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so these places were where you worked and lived at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, you were transported to these--

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: --places And not everybody was in the same place. I did cleanup work. And then there was Neuengraben. That was a new location. And there you had two factories, one was called Schindler. I don't think it's that Schindler. And the other one was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we went to Harburg, which they were really related. And so there was Hamburg-Harburg. Harburg was also a harbor. And there they had the sportplatz. And the last one was called-- that one I'll never forget. It was horrible. Tiefstack. There was a cement factory where people worked under terrible conditions. It was really bad. The-- but they-- they made us get up at night and go to the shelters. They didn't want us to be blown up for some reason. And they located us near strategic manufacturing, and hoping that the-- that they wouldn't be bombed because of it. So all the important factories for the war effort were near us. That's why we had the bombs. Even is we weren't hit, we were shaken and they dragged us out during the night to go to it. And under threat of punishment if you don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where are we? We're going towards Bergen-Belsen. They moved us in that direction. We didn't know it yet. We didn't know what Bergen-- So this-- this has all been 1944 already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Into 1945 already. We are now probably in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is over a year basically--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --all these different places?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a little over a year. Yes. Every few months they moved us. And sometimes conditions were not bad. Sometimes they were terrible. And we had, for example, I mean, you keep your sense of humor, and you-- you find things to laugh about. Otherwise, you can't exist. I remember one little incident, maybe very foolish to even bring up. There was this little man, this SS man, who, when he addressed us in order to give us direction and scare us a little bit before we marched out to our work, he stood up on a chair. And he belted out these threats to us because the-- for example, one day before this particular speech, we women were resting somewhere near an-- near a garden. And there were-- there was an apple tree, and the branches were hanging over. The apples were ripe, so some of the women climbed up, two of them, three of them, climbed up and cleaned out that branch. And we all ate apples. And that was forbidden. First of all, it was German property. Secondly, we were prisoners and had no right to do that. And the way he billed it up, I fell apart laughing. It was so comical.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, I spoke to the owner of this garden. And what does he tell me? In German, of course, he spoke. What does he tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He looks outside. [SPEAKING GERMAN] It means Jew women, not Jewish women, that is refined. Jew women is derogatory. Jew women on the apple trees. And I had this picture suddenly in my mind of us Jew women growing out of-- of these trees and producing. And I thought it was, here is this little man yelling at us to that we ate those apples. And these Jewish women on-- on-- growing out of the apple trees. I just couldn't help laughing. My mother said, stop laughing, he'll see you. It's not worth it. I can't stop. I can't. I said, look. I'm growing out of the pear tree this time. And it-- so we laughed. We did laugh. We had to laugh, and-- and we had so many talented people that spoke about literature to us at night when was in our own room, who spoke about art, and who-- and all their experiences. My mother spoke about the first war. And there were not many mothers left at that point. There were only two. And in the end, I think she was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was, if my father was 49, she was 48 at that point. She was having a tough time. And we-- we inched our way little by little, various means of transportation, because they needed the trucks, and they needed the-- they were doing badly. And we rarely-- we rarely have so much-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Anyway, we arrived on the-- we left Germany-- I mean, these German camps, on the 7th of April, 1945. And we started by truck, and by various means, to go further inland. So I don't know exactly when we arrived in Bergen-Belsen. It's-- if we left on the 7th, we-- it may have taken two or three days to get there. But when we arrived there, that was the horror of horrors. There was a-- an epidemic of typhoid going on. If you ever saw newsreels with piles of bodies and utter misery. I mean, beyond description. We arrived. We felt like we were, you know, in good shape compared to these people. They were lying on the ground, half alive, half dead, whatever, without any attention given to them whatsoever. It was covered with people. Inside, outside everywhere. And my sister claims we were in-- in Bergen-Belsen for a week. I think it was three days. I mean I just don't-- it was all one big blur. The-- obviously, the Nazis disappeared, our overseers, they-- they-- they ran away. So we knew that the Allies, somebody is approaching in this direction. They contaminated the water. They poisoned the water--

speaker: interviewer
text: Purposely.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before they left. On purpose, yes. The-- there was one SS woman hiding out in a bunk under somebody's bed, whom they got rid of. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they killed her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether they did, or whether she had befriended some women and that they helped her and they just simply let her go. I don't know what it was. But we were alone. When the Allies, the British army, came, we heard them from a distance already. And they arrived. They looked great to us. They brought a tank of water. We-- we couldn't drink the water. We had no food. They arrived, and they were so shocked that some-- some of them could not-- could not function. And they spoke through the bullhorn, you're free now. Don't panic. Don't do this. You know, the usual instructions you would give people that are-- that we have-- we have food for you. But be slow about eating it, and all that. We knew that. Mother had told us already, don't eat much. Just taste something, put it away. And they-- they were just great. They were. When they were replaced a few weeks later, it was no comparison. They had-- they-- they were just unbelievably wonderful to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- one of the officers there came over to me, he talked to others too, and he said, what would you like? What can I do for you? If you want a bike, I'll go to the next German village.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll take-- I'll find the nicest bike and it's yours. I said, I don't want their bike. I don't want anything. We are-- when they wanted to know what we really want, I said, you have to find out about our father. But they-- they were very well organized. They provided all the necessities and tried to-- they brought doctors. They brought everything. And we were afraid to lie down or to sit, or to do anything, because of the typhoid, which I contacted and my mother too-- my mother first. And I got it then. She survived the typhoid too. At-- and my sister, speaking English, and fairly good at it, was of great help to them. She translated. She was the translator in-- in some of the cases. For example, they wanted to find out first what happened to the water, what they put in the water. So they-- they caught some of the SS people. And here was this SS man in his uniform, in his boots, and crouched before her, begging, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means gracious, Fraulein. Don't get me wrong, I didn't do--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was to your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my sister. She translated to the British about the water. He doesn't know what was in the water. He didn't, nobody knew anything. They didn't see anything. They didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the first Friday that we were there, it was the next day or the two days later, my mother said to Gita, my sister, look, you speak English. In other words, I taught you. You go and make some use of it. Find the rabbi. There has to be-- every army, she said, has a chaplain. Oh, among the British, find a rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Find the rabbi. Tell him that your mother is here and that I want candles for Shabbas. And I want a seder. I haven't seen a seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm going to cut you off now for a minute.
